text: We propose the application of single layer and deep convolutional networks for remote sensing data analysis. The application of supervised (shallow or deep) convolutional networks to multi- and hyper-spectral remote sensing data is not straight-forward, due to the high dimensional input space and limited amount of available training data. To address these difficulties, we propose a greedy layer-wise unsupervised pre-training followed by an efficient unsupervised algorithm for learning sparse features. Our algorithm is based on the principles of sparse coding and it enforces population and lifetime sparsity of the features. We show the effectiveness of the extracted features in several classification applications such as aerial scene classification and land-use classification from very high resolution (VHR) images, as well as land-cover classification from multi- and hyper-spectral data. The proposed algorithm outperforms by a large margin standard approaches such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and its kernel version, (kPCA), and also outperforms current state-of-the-art classification methods for aerial images, while at the same time being extremely efficient. We show that single layer convolutional networks can extract highly discriminative features only when the small receptive field takes into account neighboring pixels, and are preferred in classification applications that require high resolution and detailed results. Deep network architectures on the other hand, significantly outperform the single layer variants by extracting at each layer different abstraction levels of features.
headline: Fully Human Written
